Author: Zhimin Zhao
Title: When Code Becomes Cheaper Than Debugging: The Rise of Ephemeral Software Engineering
Text:
When Code Becomes Cheaper Than Debugging: The Rise of Ephemeral Software Engineering
TL;DR: Software is becoming disposable. When AI agents can rebuild an application in minutes instead of months, the economics flip: rebuilding from scratch becomes cheaper than debugging. This enables “ephemeral software” — code generated just-in-time for specific needs, used once, then deleted. Most database clusters are now AI-created and exist for mere minutes before vanishing. This inverts traditional development assumptions: single-use becomes fine, optimize for now not later, no maintenance because you just regenerate when things change.
The SDLC is being rewritten around multi-agent platforms. Human engineers shift from writing code to specification, orchestration, and validation. This raises uncomfortable questions: Why pay for SaaS when users can generate custom apps on-demand? The real paradigm shift inverts Linus Torvalds: “code is cheap, show me the talk.” Specifications and tests become the durable artifacts while implementations become fluid and disposable. We’re entering Specification-Driven Development where the challenge moves from writing maintainable code to writing maintainable specifications that generate correct code.
More than 90% of new TiDB Cloud clusters are now created by AI agents, not humans. These databases manage millions of schemas and execution environments that exist for mere minutes before vanishing. This isn’t happening at some research lab — it’s production infrastructure at a major cloud database provider, and it points to something bigger changing in how we think about software.
The economics tell the story: AI agents’ capabilities are doubling every 4–7 months. Current models achieve almost 100% success rates on tasks that take humans less than 4 minutes, according to recent METR research. If you extrapolate this, we’re looking at AI agents capable of automating a month of human software development before 2032—maybe as early as 2027 if the pace holds.
What happens when rebuilding software from scratch becomes cheaper than debugging it? This compression from human-months to agent-minutes does more than speed up development. It enables something different: ephemeral software engineering.
Disposable Code
OpenAI scientist Sean Grove has been working on this with his Conjure project, building UIs that “build themselves as ephemeral, on-demand, iterable programs that appear out of nowhere to be used, then disappear when they’re no longer needed.” He thinks the future of UI development breaks down roughly like this: 10% fixed, hand-built UIs, 40% conversational interfaces (think ChatGPT), and 50% long-tail, on-the-fly UIs generated for one-time use.
But it goes beyond UIs. Tom Occhino, Chief Product Officer at Vercel, calls this “disposable app”—using AI to “instantly generate software with sufficient completeness, skip maintenance, and discard it once it has served its purpose.” Code gets created in real-time, used once, deleted. Developers talk about “just-in-time programs that serve the purpose on the spot,” where you don’t search for existing solutions, you just generate what you need and swap it out when requirements change.
The evidence is already showing up in production. That TiDB stat I mentioned earlier —90% of new clusters created by AI agents—means databases are managing millions of schemas and execution environments that last minutes. AI agents are ephemeral by design, spun up for specific tasks, then torn down.
How This Changes Development
Traditional software development assumes you’re building for permanence. Months to build means it needs to last years. High switching costs mean you plan carefully upfront. Long maintenance cycles mean technical debt piles up. Ephemeral software inverts this. Minutes to build means single-use is fine. Near-zero rebuild cost means you optimize for now. No maintenance — just regenerate when things change.
The whole software development life cycle (SDLC)is getting rewritten. AI agents are moving from copilots to autonomous builders, and development is converging into multi-agent platforms where specialized agents handle design, code, test, and deployment in integrated workflows. Planning that used to take months now takes minutes. Design used to optimize for 5-year lifespans — now it optimizes for current needs and you rebuild when requirements shift. Implementation that needed teams coordinating for weeks now happens through multi-agent ecosystems with specialized roles: Frontend Specialists, Backend Architects, Security Guardians, Performance Optimizers. Testing cycles give way to instant validation. Releases become continuous deployment of micro-changes. And here’s the weird part — years of bug fixes get replaced by a cost calculation: is it cheaper to rebuild than debug?
The market’s betting this is real. The generative AI in SDLC market is projected to jump from $0.25 billion in 2025 to $75.3 billion by 2035—a 300x increase. 68% of organizations are already using or planning to use generative and agentic AI in development.
What This Means
Some uncomfortable questions come up. If users can generate custom apps on-demand in minutes, why pay monthly fees for generic SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)? Ephemeral apps and instant-generated interfaces might outnumber traditional SaaS by orders of magnitude. Gartner predicts that by end of 2026, 40% of enterprise apps will integrate task-specific AI agents — up from less than 5% in 2025. By 2030, 35% of point-product SaaS tools will be replaced or absorbed by AI agent ecosystems. The first casualties will be thin SaaS products — basic form builders, simple schedulers, generic dashboards — anything an AI can regenerate on demand in minutes.
Right now 76% of developers refactor at least half of AI-generated code before using it. The Stack Overflow 2025 Developer Survey paints a sharper picture: 80% of developers use AI tools, but trust in AI accuracy has dropped from 40% to just 29%. The #1 frustration, cited by 45% of respondents, is “AI solutions that are almost right but not quite” — which often costs more time than writing from scratch. But when rebuild cost hits zero, does “good enough for now” become the standard? Does disposability mean lower quality, or does rapid iteration improve things? Human engineers will probably shift toward high-level strategy, creative problem-solving, and managing AI agent ecosystems instead of writing code directly. The skill set moves from implementation to specification, orchestration, validation.
There are security implications too. AI agents running with delegated authority and cross-domain access create new challenges around transparency, interpretability, and ethical use — especially when the software itself keeps regenerating.
The Real Shift
Linus Torvalds famously said “talk is cheap, show me the code.” In a world where AI agents can generate implementations in minutes, we might need to invert this: “code is cheap, show me the talk.” When rebuild cost approaches zero, the specification becomes the constant while implementations become fluid and disposable. This is getting formalized as Specification-Driven Development (SDD)—executable specifications (requirements, tests, behavioral descriptions) become the primary artifacts, with AI agents generating implementations on demand. Requirements and tests become what lasts in a world where code is ephemeral. The challenge moves from “how do we write maintainable code?” to “how do we write maintainable specifications that generate correct code?”
But specifications alone don’t capture everything. A recent ICSE 2026 paper argues that as automated generation makes code production cheap and disposable, the scarce resource shifts to human judgment — deciding what systems should exist, governing how they’re generated, and continuously verifying they behave as intended. The authors call this “accountability collapse”: the erosion of links between human decisions and system behavior when automated synthesis determines software structure. In ephemeral software, this problem intensifies — if code is regenerated constantly, who is responsible when it misbehaves?
This is already visible in practice. McKinsey’s 2025 State of AI report found that over 90% of software teams use AI for core engineering tasks, saving an average of six hours per week. But the gap between leaders and laggards is stark: top-performing teams achieved 16–30% improvements in productivity and 31–45% in software quality, while AI high-performers are 3x more likely to scale agent usage. The difference isn’t whether teams use AI — it’s whether they have the judgment infrastructure to govern what AI produces. Small teams with strong judgment are already outperforming large teams without it: Cursor reached $500M ARR with ~20 people, Midjourney hit $200M ARR with 10.
We’re not just automating software development. Software itself is becoming ephemeral, disposable, generated just-in-time for specific needs. The implications for how we build, sell, and consume software go pretty deep. The question isn’t whether this happens — it’s whether organizations are ready for a world where software gets measured in regenerations, not releases.
Three questions worth asking yourself:
1. If your codebase vanished tomorrow, what would you actually need to recreate it? If the answer is “the code,” you’re in trouble. If it’s “our specifications, tests, and domain knowledge,” you’re already thinking in ephemeral terms.
2. What percentage of your engineering time goes to maintaining code versus defining intent? As rebuild costs approach zero, the ratio should flip. Organizations still spending 80% on maintenance are subsidizing a world that’s disappearing.
3. Could a 10-person team with AI agents replace your software product? If the answer is yes, your moat isn’t code — and you need to figure out what it actually is before the market does it for you.
